advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Restrict environment refutation to references that carry a container/dotted-module qualifier (or an `import` kind), not bare single-segment `symbol` names that can collide with stdlib/installed module names.
evidence: resolve.py:603-605: `env = _refute_via_environment(ref)` runs on zero repo defs for any symbol/import name
environment.py:70-71: a bare `nm` is tried as a top-level module — `resolve_in_environment("queue", language="python")` returns a hit because stdlib `queue` is importable

refute_via_environment builds a `refuted` record for any importable name; the reviewer's claim 'symbol X does not exist in the code' is about a repo symbol, so refuting it on a stdlib-module collision is a false refute

ABSENCE: no test exercises `{"kind":"symbol","name":"queue"}` (or any stdlib-name collision as a symbol) — tests/unit/grounding/test_environment_resolution.py only covers pydantic/json existence, unknown names, and members
advisory.finding: A bare `kind=symbol`/`kind=import` reference whose name collides with an installed or stdlib module (e.g. `json`, `queue`, `types`, `logging`, `email`, `secrets`) is REFUTED by the environment lane even when the reviewer's asserted-absence was about a repo-level symbol, not that module — converting a valid finding into a false refute.
scenarios: Plan/code reviewer asserts a repo helper named `secrets`/`queue`/`types` is missing; the oracle refutes the absence because a same-named stdlib module imports

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm importing installed packages for attribute checks is acceptable in the grounding path, or gate member resolution to trusted modules / use a non-executing check.
evidence: environment.py:104-108: `mod = importlib.import_module(module)` inside `_attribute_exists`
environment.py:66-69: for a dotted `name` the head (`module.attr` split) is imported to check the attr
docstring at environment.py:50-53 acknowledges 'an attribute bind imports the module (running its package __init__)'
advisory.finding: Member/import resolution imports the target module (`importlib.import_module`) which executes arbitrary package `__init__` (and any dotted-head package) code from whatever is installed in the environment, during a review/grounding pass driven by reviewer-supplied reference names.
scenarios: A reference like `some_installed_pkg.Thing` triggers a real import of `some_installed_pkg`, running its top-level side effects in the grounding process

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test pinning the desired behavior for a bare `kind=symbol` name that collides with a stdlib/installed module (abstain vs. refute decision).
evidence: Tests cover only: pydantic import/member refuted, json refuted, container+name, unknown-module abstain, missing-member abstain, local receiver None, non-python None, injection None
ABSENCE: no assertion that `{"kind":"symbol","name":"<stdlib-name>"}` still abstains when the reviewer meant a repo symbol
advisory.finding: The commit claims 'FALSE-REFUTES = 0' but the new tests lack any negative case for the symbol/module name-collision path (a repo symbol name that shadows a stdlib/installed module), leaving the primary false-refute vector uncovered.
scenarios: Regression guard for the collision case is missing, so a future change (or the current behavior) can false-refute undetected

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a resolve_symbol test that resolves a repo-local module (origin under repo_path) and asserts the output reports `repo-local` scope.
evidence: pai_tools.py:180-181: `inside = _within_root(os.path.realpath(origin), root) ...` then `scope = "repo-local" if inside else "third-party/stdlib (site-packages)"`
All tool tests resolve only site-packages symbols: test_resolve_symbol_confirms_third_party_module asserts `"third-party/stdlib" in out`; no test asserts a `repo-local` result
ABSENCE: no test passes a name/module resolving to an origin inside `root` (e.g. a rebar module under repo_path) to cover the inside=True path
advisory.finding: The resolve_symbol tool's 'repo-local' scope branch (inside=True, via _within_root) is never exercised: all tests use pydantic/BaseModel/json which always resolve under site-packages, so only the 'third-party/stdlib' branch is tested. A regression inverting the scope classification or breaking the realpath/_within_root wiring would mislabel a repo-local symbol to the agent yet pass every test.
scenarios: A future edit inverts the `repo-local`/`third-party` ternary or breaks _within_root integration and no test catches it
The agent is told a genuinely repo-local symbol is 'third-party/stdlib' (or vice versa)
advisory.suggested_fix: Call grounding_tools(<rebar repo root>) and resolve_symbol('rebar.grounding.resolve') (or a known in-repo module), asserting the output starts with 'EXISTS' and contains 'repo-local'.

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a runner-level test asserting the reviewer's assembled tool list includes `resolve_symbol` (grounding_tools is wired in).
evidence: runner.py:239-245: `tools = ( pai_tools.filesystem_tools(cfg.repo_path) + pai_tools.grounding_tools(cfg.repo_path) + pai_tools.rebar_tools(...) )`
search_files 'grounding_tools' under tests/ returns only hits inside test_resolve_symbol_tool.py (direct tool-function tests), none exercising runner.py's tool assembly

Ticket 406f acceptance criterion: 'A regression test injects a plan referencing a known-importable third-party symbol and asserts no absence-BLOCK is produced' — the runner wiring is the path that satisfies it
advisory.finding: The runner.py wiring that actually adds grounding_tools/resolve_symbol to the reviewer's tool list is untested. This is the integration point the bug fix depends on and the ticket's regression-test acceptance criterion targets, yet no test asserts the reviewer's assembled tool set includes resolve_symbol.
scenarios: A future refactor of runner.py drops the `+ pai_tools.grounding_tools(cfg.repo_path)` term; all existing tests stay green while the original false-BLOCK bug reopens
advisory.suggested_fix: Exercise the runner tool-assembly path (or extract it into a testable helper) and assert `'resolve_symbol' in [t.__name__ for t in tools]`.

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Constrain environment member resolution so untrusted names cannot trigger importlib.import_module of arbitrary installed packages (e.g. restrict to an allowlist / declared project deps, or avoid actual import when checking attribute existence).
evidence: environment.py:104-108: `try:\n    mod = importlib.import_module(module)\n... return hasattr(mod, attr)` — import_module runs top-level/package __init__ code
environment.py:78: member path calls `_attribute_exists(mod, attr)` for any candidate whose module resolves

environment.py:35: `_IMPORTABLE_NAME_RE = re.compile(r"^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*(?:\.[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*)*$")` — permits any valid dotted module name (e.g. `antigravity`, an installed side-effectful package); it does not restrict WHICH module is imported
pai_tools.py:168: `loc = _resolve.resolve_in_environment(name, container=module or None, language="python")` — agent-supplied `name`/`module` flow straight to the resolver with no allowlist
resolve.py:568 (prior T1 behavior) only does `defs = index.lookup(name)` — a static ctags index lookup with NO import; the import sink is newly introduced by this change
advisory.finding: New taint-to-sink path: untrusted (LLM/plan-extracted) symbol names reach importlib.import_module, which executes the target module's import-time code inside the reviewer process. The identifier regex blocks shell metacharacters but not the name of any installed module, so requesting a member on a side-effectful/malicious installed package runs its __init__ code.
scenarios: Reviewer LLM (processing an attacker-authored diff/plan) calls resolve_symbol with module set to an installed package whose __init__ has side effects (network, thread spawn, file writes), executing that code in the reviewer process
A plan/code-extracted `kind=member` reference like `somepkg.attr` routed through oracle.refute_absence triggers import of `somepkg`

coaching.coaching: Threat-model the change to import-time code execution via untrusted symbol names (trust boundary, input validation).
coaching.subject: import-time code execution via untrusted symbol names
